title: 10 Soothing Herbal Teas for Pertussis Sufferers
intro_desc: Herbal teas for pertussis are a natural remedy that helps alleviate symptoms of this contagious respiratory illness. They work by boosting the immune system and reducing inflammation. The best herbal teas for pertussis include Echinacea purpurea, Echinacea angustifolia, Zingiber officinale, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Echinacea pallida, Eucalyptus globulus, Thymus serpyllum, Lavandula angustifolia, Pelargonium sidoides, and Astragalus membranaceus. If left untreated, pertussis can lead to severe coughing fits, bronchitis, and pneumonia, causing fatigue, missed work or school, and strained relationships with loved ones.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea tea has been used to help with pertussis, also known as whooping cough. This tea is made from the roots of the Echinacea purpurea plant, which has been used in traditional medicine for many years. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity of whooping cough symptoms. Echinacea purpurea tea may also boost the immune system, which can help the body fight off the bacteria that causes pertussis. Additionally, the tea has been shown to have antiviral properties, which can help prevent the spread of the illness. Some people also believe that Echinacea purpurea tea can help reduce coughing and fever, which are common symptoms of whooping cough. Studies have found that Echinacea purpurea may be most effective when used in the early stages of whooping cough, before the symptoms become severe. More research is needed to fully understand the effects of Echinacea purpurea tea on whooping cough, but some people find it to be a helpful natural remedy.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Cichoric acid is good for pertussis because it has antimicrobial properties that help fight off the bacteria that cause whooping cough, reducing the severity and duration of the illness.

Flavonoids are good for pertussis because they have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity of coughs and fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that cause whooping cough.
Alkylamides are good for pertussis because they have antimicrobial properties that help fight off the bacteria that cause whooping cough, reducing the severity and duration of the illness.
Tannins are good for pertussis because they have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity and duration of whooping cough symptoms.

remedies.image_alt: Echinacea purpurea herbal teas for pertussis
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Thymus serpyllum herbal tea that may help with pertussis, you will need fresh or dried Thyme leaves. First, gather the necessary ingredients: 1 tablespoon of Thyme leaves for every 8 ounces of water. If using fresh Thyme, chop the leaves and stems finely. If using dried Thyme, skip this step. Next, bring the water to a boil in a pot, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Add the chopped or dried Thyme leaves to the water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbs to release their healing properties. You can strain the tea using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup or teapot. Finally, drink the tea warm, ideally 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion. It is essential to note that while Thymus serpyllum herbal tea may offer some relief, it should not replace medical treatment. Consult a healthcare professional before using herbal remedies, especially if you have any underlying health conditions or are pregnant/breastfeeding.

remedies.remedy_desc: Eucalyptus globulus tea is a popular remedy for pertussis, a contagious respiratory illness also known as whooping cough. This tea is made from the leaves of the eucalyptus tree and contains a compound called eucalyptol, which has natural decongestant and anti-inflammatory properties. When you drink the tea, the eucalyptol helps to loosen and clear mucus from your airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing the risk of complications. The tea also has a soothing effect on the throat and lungs, helping to calm coughing fits and ease the discomfort associated with pertussis. Additionally, eucalyptus globulus tea has antimicrobial properties, which can help to fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that cause the illness.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Quercetin is good for pertussis because it has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation and fight off infections in the respiratory system.
Cineole is good for pertussis because it has antimicrobial properties that help stop the Bordetella pertussis bacteria from growing and multiplying, reducing the severity of the disease.
Eucalyptol has been shown to be good for pertussis because its decongestant properties help to break down mucus in the lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing the severity of coughing fits.
Tannins have antimicrobial properties that help to fight the bacteria Bordetella pertussis, which causes pertussis, by reducing the severity and duration of the illness.
Polyphenols have been found to be good for pertussis because they help reduce inflammation in the airways, making it easier for the body to fight off the bacteria that cause whooping cough.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Thymus serpyllum herbal teas that help with pertussis, first, you need to gather Thymus serpyllum leaves and flowers. Thymus serpyllum is also known as creeping thyme. You can find it in gardens or purchase it from a health food store. Next, dry the leaves and flowers to make them ready for use. To dry, simply tie them in small bunches and hang them upside down in a warm, dry place. Once they are dry, you can use a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle to crush them into a fine powder. Now, to make the tea, boil some water and add one teaspoon of the crushed Thymus serpyllum to the boiling water. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea and drink it while it's still warm. You can repeat this process several times a day to help relieve the symptoms of pertussis. It is essential to note that Thymus serpyllum is not a replacement for medical treatment, but it may help alleviate some symptoms. Consult a doctor or healthcare professional before using it as a treatment for pertussis.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tea, also known as licorice root tea, has been used to help treat pertussis, or whooping cough. This is because the tea contains compounds that can help soothe and calm the throat and respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. Glycyrrhizin, a key component of the tea, has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce swelling and irritation in the airways. This can be particularly helpful for people with pertussis, as the condition often causes severe coughing fits that can lead to inflammation and congestion. Additionally, the tea's expectorant properties can help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and reducing the risk of complications. By reducing inflammation and helping to clear mucus, Glycyrrhiza glabra tea may provide relief from the symptoms of pertussis and help the body recover more quickly.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Glycyrrhizin is good for pertussis because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity of the coughing fits associated with whooping cough.

Flavonoids are good for pertussis because they have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity of coughing and make it easier for the body to fight off the bacteria that causes whooping cough.
Polyphenols are good for pertussis because they have antibacterial properties that help fight the Bordetella pertussis bacteria causing the disease, thus reducing its severity and duration.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Thymus serpyllum herbal tea that can help with pertussis, start by gathering dried thyme leaves and flowers. You can find dried Thymus serpyllum at health food stores or online. Next, boil a cup of water and then remove it from the heat. Add one teaspoon of the dried thyme to the water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. This will allow the active compounds in the thyme to infuse into the water. Strain the liquid into a cup and discard the thyme leaves. You can add honey or lemon to taste, if desired. Thymus serpyllum has antibacterial properties that can help combat the bacteria that cause pertussis, also known as whooping cough. Drinking this tea may help alleviate symptoms and reduce the duration of the illness. It's essential to note that while herbal teas like Thymus serpyllum can be helpful in managing symptoms, they should not be used as a replacement for medical treatment. Consult with a healthcare professional before using this tea, especially if you are pregnant, breastfeeding, or have any underlying health conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tea, also known as ginger tea, has been used to help with pertussis, also known as whooping cough. This illness is caused by a type of bacteria and can make people cough a lot. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce the swelling in the throat and chest, making it easier to breathe and cough. It also has antibacterial properties that help to fight off the infection that causes pertussis. Furthermore, ginger tea has a soothing effect on the digestive system, which can be affected by the vomiting and diarrhea that often come with whooping cough. Additionally, ginger has natural expectorant properties, helping to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing the severity of coughing fits. By helping to reduce the symptoms of pertussis, ginger tea can help people feel more comfortable and recover faster from the illness.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol has been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion, by reducing the growth of the Bordetella pertussis bacteria and decreasing inflammation in the respiratory tract.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thymus vulgaris tea, also known as thyme tea, is a natural remedy that may help with pertussis, commonly known as whooping cough. This tea contains a compound called thymol, which is known for its antibacterial and antiviral properties. These properties can help fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that cause whooping cough. When we drink thyme tea, the thymol in it can help reduce inflammation and kill the bacteria in the body, making it harder for the disease to spread. Additionally, thyme tea has expectorant properties, which means it can help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to breathe. This can be especially helpful for people with whooping cough, who often experience severe coughing fits and difficulty getting air.

Start by boiling a cup of water and then adding a teaspoon of fresh Thyme leaves or a tablespoon of dried Thyme leaves to it. Allow the mixture to steep for 5 to 10 minutes, which will help the active ingredients to release into the water. Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the leaves, and then discard them. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be cautious not to add too much sugar as it may counteract the benefits of the tea. Once the tea has cooled down, you can drink it as needed to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion. It is essential to note that while Thymus serpyllum herbal tea may provide relief, it is not a substitute for medical treatment, and you should consult a healthcare professional before using it to treat pertussis.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea angustifolia tea is used to help with pertussis, also known as whooping cough. This illness causes a bad cough that can make it hard to breathe. It has properties that can help reduce inflammation and fight off the bacteria that cause pertussis. Drinking Echinacea angustifolia tea may help ease the symptoms of pertussis, such as the cough and fever. It may also help boost the immune system, which can help the body fight off the infection. Some people believe that Echinacea angustifolia tea can help reduce the severity and duration of pertussis. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Thymus serpyllum herbal teas that can help with pertussis, start by gathering 1 teaspoon of dried Thyme leaves. You can dry the leaves yourself by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. If using fresh leaves, use 2 teaspoons. Next, boil 1 cup of water in a saucepan. Then, remove the boiling water from the heat and add the dried or fresh Thyme leaves. Allow the leaves to steep for 5-7 minutes, depending on your desired strength of tea. After the steeping time has passed, strain the tea into a cup using a piece of cheesecloth or a tea infuser to catch the leaves. Once the tea has cooled slightly, you can drink it as is, or add honey to taste. It's recommended to drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion. However, consult with a healthcare professional before using Thymus serpyllum tea as a treatment for pertussis, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking any medications.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis tea, also known as rosemary tea, is a herbal remedy that has been used to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, also known as whooping cough. This is because rosemary tea contains compounds that have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to combat the bacteria that cause pertussis. The tea's antimicrobial properties can help to reduce the severity of the cough and other respiratory symptoms associated with the illness. Additionally, rosemary tea has been traditionally used to ease congestion and soothe a sore throat, which can be particularly helpful during a bout of whooping cough. The anti-inflammatory properties in rosemary tea may also help to reduce the inflammation and irritation in the airways that can make breathing difficult. As a natural remedy, rosemary tea may be a useful addition to conventional treatment for pertussis, especially when used in conjunction with medical care.

remedies.remedy_desc: Sambucus nigra tea, also known as elderberry tea, has been used for centuries to help with colds and coughs. For pertussis, also known as whooping cough, this tea can be very helpful. The flowers and berries of the Sambucus nigra plant contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties. These properties can help reduce the severity of symptoms such as a persistent cough, fever, and congestion. The tea can also help soothe the throat and calm the cough, making it easier to breathe and sleep. Additionally, Sambucus nigra tea has been shown to have immune-boosting properties, which can help the body fight off the bacteria that cause pertussis. By drinking Sambucus nigra tea regularly, some people may find that their symptoms become less severe and they recover faster.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Thymus serpyllum herbal tea that helps with pertussis, also known as whooping cough, start by gathering fresh or dried Thymus serpyllum leaves. You can find this herb in health food stores or grow it yourself in your garden. If using fresh leaves, rinse them with clean water and pat them dry with a paper towel. If using dried leaves, skip this step. Next, place one teaspoon of Thymus serpyllum leaves in a tea infuser or a small muslin bag. Pour one cup of boiling water over the leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea into a cup and discard the leaves. Let the tea cool down for a minute or two before drinking. Thymus serpyllum tea has antibacterial properties that can help fight the bacteria causing pertussis, and it may also help reduce the severity of symptoms such as coughing and fever. However, it's essential to consult a healthcare professional before using this tea as a treatment for pertussis, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking any medications.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hydrastis canadensis tea, also known as goldenseal, has been used to help fight off pertussis, a serious respiratory infection also known as whooping cough. The tea contains a compound called berberine, which has strong antibacterial properties that can help kill the bacteria that cause pertussis. When you drink Hydrastis canadensis tea, the berberine goes into your body and starts to fight off the infection. It can also help to reduce the severity of the symptoms, such as a persistent cough, and may even help to reduce the number of times you cough. Additionally, Hydrastis canadensis tea has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce the swelling in the lungs and airways that can make pertussis so uncomfortable. Drinking Hydrastis canadensis tea regularly may also help to boost your immune system, making it easier for your body to fight off the infection.

Start by boiling a cup of water in a pot and then add a tablespoon of dried thyme leaves or a handful of fresh leaves, whichever you have available. Next, reduce the heat to a simmer for about 5-7 minutes, allowing the thyme to infuse into the water and release its medicinal properties. After this time, strain the tea into a cup using a piece of cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to catch the leaves. You can then drink the tea hot or let it cool down to a comfortable temperature before consuming it. It's recommended to drink 2-3 cups of Thymus serpyllum tea per day to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion. However, please note that this herbal remedy is not a substitute for medical treatment and you should consult a healthcare professional before using it as a treatment for pertussis.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thymus serpyllum tea, also known as wild thyme tea, has been used for centuries to help treat respiratory issues. The tea is made from the leaves of the thyme plant, which contains a compound called thymol. Thymol has antimicrobial properties, which means it can help fight off infections and reduce inflammation in the lungs. When it comes to pertussis, also known as whooping cough, Thymus serpyllum tea may be beneficial because it can help alleviate the cough and reduce the severity of the infection. The tea may also help to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to breathe. Additionally, thyme's antimicrobial properties may help to prevent the spread of the infection to others. Drinking Thymus serpyllum tea may also help to soothe a sore throat and reduce the discomfort associated with pertussis.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Thymol is good for pertussis because it has antimicrobial properties that help fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria, which causes the disease.

Rosmarinic acid is good for pertussis because it has strong antioxidant properties that help reduce inflammation and fight off bacteria that cause the disease, thus potentially helping to prevent or treat whooping cough.

Carvacrol is good for pertussis because it has antibacterial properties that can help kill the Bordetella pertussis bacteria, which cause the infection, and also has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce the severity of symptoms.

remedies.image_alt: Thymus serpyllum herbal teas for pertussis
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Thymus serpyllum herbal tea that can help with pertussis, start by gathering fresh or dried Thymus serpyllum leaves. If using fresh leaves, pick a handful and rinse them gently under cold water. If using dried leaves, simply measure out the required amount, usually 1-2 teaspoons per cup of boiling water. Next, boil fresh water in a teapot or kettle and pour it over the Thymus serpyllum leaves. Let it steep for 5-10 minutes, allowing the leaves to release their active compounds into the water. You can speed up the process by letting it sit for a shorter time, but be aware that the tea might not be as potent. Once the steeping time is up, strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth to catch the leaves. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to drink the tea while it's still warm, as the heat can help to release the medicinal properties of the Thymus serpyllum. It's recommended to drink 2-3 cups of this herbal tea per day to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis and support the immune system. However, please consult a healthcare professional before using Thymus serpyllum herbal tea as a treatment, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications.

supplementary_best: To remedy pertussis, a combination of Echinacea angustifolia, Thymus serpyllum, and Zingiber officinale is recommended. Echinacea angustifolia is chosen for its immune-boosting properties, which can help combat the infection. Thymus serpyllum, also known as wild thyme, has antimicrobial properties that can help fight the bacteria causing pertussis, and its expectorant properties can aid in relieving coughs. Zingiber officinale, or ginger, is added for its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe the respiratory tract and reduce congestion. This combination works well together because Echinacea angustifolia can enhance Thymus serpyllum's antimicrobial effects, while Zingiber officinale can help reduce inflammation caused by the infection.
supplementary_avoid: If you have pertussis, a contagious respiratory illness, it's essential to be cautious with herbal teas that may worsen symptoms or interact with your treatment. Avoid peppermint tea (Mentha piperita) as it may cause stomach upset, which can be a problem if you're already experiencing nausea or vomiting due to the illness. Sage tea (Salvia officinalis) is also not recommended as it can dry out your mouth and throat, making it harder to cough up mucus. Licorice root tea (Glycyrrhiza uralensis), although not the same as licorice root, has similar properties that may interact with your medications or make your condition worse. Yarrow tea (Achillea millefolium) may cause allergic reactions or interact with your medications, which could be problematic. Some herbal teas, like black cohosh tea (Actaea racemosa), may slow down your heart rate, which is not safe if you're experiencing breathing difficulties due to pertussis. These teas should be avoided until your symptoms improve and you consult with your doctor or healthcare provider.
supplementary_other_remedies: Elderberry syrup has antiviral properties that can help fight pertussis, a highly contagious respiratory illness. It works by blocking the virus that causes the illness from spreading and making you feel worse. Taking elderberry syrup can reduce the severity of symptoms and the duration of the illness.  Slippery elm is another natural remedy that can help with pertussis. It is a type of herb that soothes and protects the mucous membranes in the throat and lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing coughing. The anti-inflammatory properties of slippery elm can also help reduce swelling and pain in the throat.  Eucalyptus oil has decongestant properties that can help relieve congestion and coughing caused by pertussis. When inhaled, eucalyptus oil can open up the airways, making it easier to breathe. It can also be added to a warm bath or applied to the chest to help loosen mucus and reduce congestion.  Licorice root has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that can help fight pertussis. It can help reduce inflammation in the throat and lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing coughing. Licorice root can also help soothe a sore throat and reduce the severity of symptoms.  Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and pain in the throat and lungs caused by pertussis. It can also help thin mucus, making it easier to cough up. Ginger can be consumed as a tea, added to food, or taken as a supplement to help alleviate symptoms.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with pertussis, also known as whooping cough, often experience other health issues. These can include coughing up mucus, which can be thick and yellowish, making it hard to breathe. This is because the bacteria that cause pertussis, Bordetella pertussis, irritate the lungs and airways, leading to inflammation and congestion.  Elderberry has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways, making it easier to breathe. The berries of the elder tree contain compounds that have been shown to reduce swelling and ease congestion.  Another issue people with pertussis may face is dehydration. The constant coughing can lead to a dry, sore throat and a decrease in fluid production, making it hard to stay hydrated.  Licorice root has been used traditionally to soothe a sore throat and promote saliva production, which can help keep the throat moist and ease dehydration. Licorice root also contains compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling in the throat and airways.  Additionally, people with pertussis may experience fever, fatigue, and headaches due to the bacterial infection.  Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce fever and alleviate headaches. Ginger also has compounds that can help stimulate digestion and reduce nausea and vomiting, which can be caused by the bacterial infection.  Finally, people with pertussis may experience sleep disturbances due to the constant coughing and difficulty breathing.  Valerian root has been used traditionally to promote relaxation and improve sleep quality. Valerian root contains compounds that can help reduce stress and anxiety, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep.  These medicinal herbs have been used traditionally to alleviate the symptoms of pertussis and its related issues.
